teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the end-to-end navigation outcome students want to achieve.
teaching_arc.progression: Move through the Nav2 architecture from inputs to decisions to motion, then connect that structure to the course project.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish with a system map they can use while building and debugging their project.

audience_level: introductory autonomous mobile robotics students learning ROS 2 through lab-first workflows
topic_goal: Give students a high-level navigation architecture map and connect it to the project work they will complete.
topic_takeaways: Students should understand the major Nav2 components and what each contributes.
Students should know how localization, planning, control, and behavior trees fit together.
Students should leave seeing the project as an integration task rather than isolated commands.
style_notes: Keep the tone hands-on and confidence-building.
Connect every concept to what students will debug in a terminal, simulation, or robot lab.
Use visualization and mental models before deep command details.
Keep the big picture visible even when naming components.
Treat the project as the synthesis point for the course.
transition_style: Move from the mental model to the concrete lab step students will run next, then close each section by explaining what to verify.

slide_ref.source_title: Navigation2 (Nav2) — How the Stack Actually Works

slides.narration_seed: This is our last lecture. Today we connect your map, localization, planning, control, and project logic into one complete mental model.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Give students a high-level navigation architecture map and connect it to the project work they will complete.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Why this session matters for your final project so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.

slides.story_hint: Keep Navigation2 (Nav2) — How the Stack Actually Works connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Navigation2 (Nav2) — How the Stack Actually Works sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slide_ref.source_title: Why this session matters for your final project

slides.narration_seed: Use Why this session matters for your final project to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: Nav2 is the ROS 2 navigation stack used after you already have a map
Your project needs more than one clicked goal — it needs programmatic behavior
Today you learn the structure: navigator, servers, plugins, parameters, costmaps, and filters
Then we connect that structure to waypoints, room logic, and restricted areas
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Navigation2 (Nav2) — How the Stack Actually Works into Why this session matters for your final project so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Where Nav2 fits in the full robot pipeline so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Why this session matters for your final project to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Why this session matters for your final project connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Why this session matters for your final project sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slide_ref.source_title: Where Nav2 fits in the full robot pipeline

slides.narration_seed: Use Where Nav2 fits in the full robot pipeline to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: 1) Build or load a map (map_server or SLAM output)
2) Localize the robot in that map (often AMCL for a saved static map)
3) Nav2 plans a path and computes safe velocity commands
4) Your application sends one goal or many goals to Nav2
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Why this session matters for your final project into Where Nav2 fits in the full robot pipeline so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Nav2 architecture: one stack, many cooperating parts so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Where Nav2 fits in the full robot pipeline to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Where Nav2 fits in the full robot pipeline connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Where Nav2 fits in the full robot pipeline sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.
slides.scene_hint: Show the robot model, TF, and RViz displays as a simple pipeline so students can connect each layer to the visual outcome.

slide_ref.source_title: Nav2 architecture: one stack, many cooperating parts

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Nav2 architecture: one stack, many cooperating parts using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Nav2 is not one magic node
The BT Navigator coordinates the task
Servers below it do planning, control, smoothing, and behaviors
Costmaps + TF + localization support all of those decisions
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into What each major Nav2 part does (Part 1) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Nav2 architecture: one stack, many cooperating parts first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Nav2 architecture: one stack, many cooperating parts connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Nav2 architecture: one stack, many cooperating parts clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slide_ref.source_title: What each major Nav2 part does (Part 1)

slides.narration_seed: Define What each major Nav2 part does (Part 1) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: BT Navigator = high-level task logic; coordinates the rest of the stack
Planner Server = computes a path from current pose to goal using the global costmap
Controller Server = follows that path and outputs velocity commands using the local costmap
Smoother Server = improves a rough path before or during execution
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What each major Nav2 part does (Part 1) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once What each major Nav2 part does (Part 1) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define What each major Nav2 part does (Part 1) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep What each major Nav2 part does (Part 1) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain What each major Nav2 part does (Part 1) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slide_ref.source_title: What each major Nav2 part does (Part 2)

slides.narration_seed: Define What each major Nav2 part does (Part 2) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Behavior Server = utility / recovery actions such as spin, backup, wait, drive-on-heading
Lifecycle Manager = starts and stops the stack in a controlled order
Map / Localization = gives Nav2 the robot pose in the map frame
Plugins = the actual algorithms loaded into planner, controller, smoother, and behavior servers
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What each major Nav2 part does (Part 2) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once What each major Nav2 part does (Part 2) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define What each major Nav2 part does (Part 2) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep What each major Nav2 part does (Part 2) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain What each major Nav2 part does (Part 2) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slide_ref.source_title: Nav2 depends on TF: map -> odom -> base_link

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Nav2 depends on TF: map -> odom -> base_link using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: The minimum useful mobile robot TF chain is:
map -> odom -> base_link -> sensor frames
map -> odom = global correction from SLAM / AMCL / localization
odom -> base_link = local motion estimate from odometry
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Saved map navigation: where AMCL fits so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Nav2 depends on TF: map -> odom -> base_link first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Nav2 depends on TF: map -> odom -> base_link clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use a TurtleBot or small car with attached coordinate frames so students can see relative transforms instead of imagining them abstractly.

slide_ref.source_title: Saved map navigation: where AMCL fits

slides.narration_seed: Define Saved map navigation: where AMCL fits in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: SLAM builds the map; AMCL localizes the robot inside that saved map
Before sending goals, you must set 2D Pose Estimate in RViz
This initializes localization so the robot aligns with the map correctly
TurtleBot3 docs explicitly require pose initialization before normal navigation
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Saved map navigation: where AMCL fits to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Saved map navigation: where AMCL fits is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Saved map navigation: where AMCL fits accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Map vs Costmap: they are not the same thing

slides.narration_seed: Compare the alternatives or concepts on Map vs Costmap: they are not the same thing clearly, emphasize the trade-off, and end with the decision rule students should remember.
must_cover: A saved map is mostly occupancy information: free / occupied / unknown
A costmap adds navigation cost information around obstacles
Global costmap supports path planning across the environment
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Local costmap supports short-range safe motion around the robot
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Saved map navigation: where AMCL fits into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Map vs Costmap: they are not the same thing explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Keep Map vs Costmap: they are not the same thing connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Map vs Costmap: they are not the same thing and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slide_ref.source_title: Typical costmap layers you should recognize

slides.narration_seed: Define Typical costmap layers you should recognize in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Static layer = the saved map or map server data
Obstacle layer = current sensor obstacles, such as LaserScan readings
Inflation layer = grows cost around obstacles to create a safety margin
These layers explain why the robot avoids walls before physically touching them
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Typical costmap layers you should recognize to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Typical costmap layers you should recognize is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slide_ref.source_title: Filters: change where the robot may go, not just how it plans

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Filters: change where the robot may go, not just how it plans using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Filters are different from normal costmap layers
Keepout filter can forbid entry to dangerous or undesirable areas
Useful project examples: table feet, narrow edges, entrance zones
A keepout mask is just another map-like image + YAML metadata
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Important keepout filter design idea so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Filters: change where the robot may go, not just how it plans first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Filters: change where the robot may go, not just how it plans clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: Important keepout filter design idea

slides.narration_seed: Define Important keepout filter design idea in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Global costmap keepout only → planner avoids those zones in the path
Local costmap keepout only → robot refuses to enter them locally, but path may still cross them
Best safety behavior usually means enabling keepout in both global and local costmaps
Keepout is realistic for your project; it is easier than full custom coverage planning
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Important keepout filter design idea to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Important keepout filter design idea is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Important keepout filter design idea in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Important keepout filter design idea accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Behavior Trees (BTs): the logic layer on top of planning and control

slides.narration_seed: Define Behavior Trees (BTs): the logic layer on top of planning and control in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: BT Navigator implements tasks like NavigateToPose and NavigateThroughPoses
A BT may compute a path, follow it, replan, and trigger recovery behaviors if needed
This is why Nav2 is flexible: task logic is separated from low-level motion logic
You do not need to write custom BT XML today — just understand the idea
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Behavior Trees (BTs): the logic layer on top of planning and control to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Behavior Trees (BTs): the logic layer on top of planning and control is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Behavior Trees (BTs): the logic layer on top of planning and control accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Planner plugins: what students should care about

slides.narration_seed: Define Planner plugins: what students should care about in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: For this course, focus on the built-in planner families you can actually configure
NavFn = classic grid planner; can use Dijkstra or A*
Smac 2D = optimized 2D cost-aware planner for common mobile robots
Theta Star = any-angle planner that can produce less grid-like paths
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Planner plugins: what students should care about to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slide_ref.source_title: How planner plugins are selected in YAML

slides.narration_seed: Define How planner plugins are selected in YAML in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Change the plugin string to switch algorithms
Then tune that planner’s own parameters in the same namespace
This is why Nav2 is flexible: same server, different planning algorithm
slides.transition_from_previous: Use How planner plugins are selected in YAML to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slide_ref.source_title: The most basic Nav2 workflow in RViz

slides.narration_seed: Define The most basic Nav2 workflow in RViz in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: 1) Launch world + Nav2 with a saved map
2) Use 2D Pose Estimate to initialize AMCL
3) Use Navigation2 Goal to send one pose goal
4) Do not keep teleop publishing cmd_vel while Nav2 is navigating
slides.transition_from_previous: Use The most basic Nav2 workflow in RViz to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slide_ref.source_title: For your project, one clicked goal is not enough

slides.narration_seed: Define For your project, one clicked goal is not enough in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: NavigateToPose = one goal pose
NavigateThroughPoses = one action request containing multiple poses
FollowWaypoints = ordered waypoint execution with progress feedback
For room-to-room tasks, waypoint-style execution is usually the easiest starting point
slides.transition_from_previous: Use For your project, one clicked goal is not enough to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once For your project, one clicked goal is not enough is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slide_ref.source_title: Why we use Nav2 Simple Commander in class

slides.narration_seed: Define Why we use Nav2 Simple Commander in class in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Simple Commander provides goToPose(), goThroughPoses(), and followWaypoints()
It also gives setInitialPose(), waitUntilNav2Active(), getFeedback(), and getResult()
This is ideal for your project because you can focus on robot behavior, not ROS plumbing
We will implement multiple goal execution using this API

slide_ref.source_title: Where the main parameter groups usually live

slides.narration_seed: Define Where the main parameter groups usually live in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: amcl: → localization behavior on a static saved map
bt_navigator: → task logic / BT settings
planner_server: → path-generation plugins and related settings
controller_server: → path-tracking behavior and checking logic
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Where the main parameter groups usually live to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Where the main parameter groups usually live is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slide_ref.source_title: Goal checker parameters you should recognize by name

slides.narration_seed: Define Goal checker parameters you should recognize by name in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: xy_goal_tolerance = how close the robot must be to the goal position in meters
yaw_goal_tolerance = how close the robot heading must be to the goal orientation in radians
path_length_tolerance = how much remaining path length is still acceptable
stateful = keeps the goal check robust after final orientation correction
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Goal checker parameters you should recognize by name to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Goal checker parameters you should recognize by name is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Goal checker parameters you should recognize by name accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Other high-value parameters worth understanding first

slides.narration_seed: Define Other high-value parameters worth understanding first in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: inflation_radius = how far obstacle cost spreads outward for safety margin
cost_scaling_factor = how sharply cost decays away from obstacles
transform_tolerance = how much TF timing lag the node can tolerate
robot_radius or footprint = what physical space the robot is assumed to occupy
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Other high-value parameters worth understanding first to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slide_ref.source_title: Examples of plugin families you will see in Nav2

slides.narration_seed: Define Examples of plugin families you will see in Nav2 in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Planner plugins choose how a path is computed, such as NavFn, Smac, or Theta*
Controller plugins choose how the robot tracks that path and generates cmd_vel
Behavior plugins provide extra actions like spin, backup, wait, or drive-on-heading
Smoother plugins improve path geometry before following it
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Examples of plugin families you will see in Nav2 to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Examples of plugin families you will see in Nav2 accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slide_ref.source_title: What about Complete Coverage Path Planning (CCPP)?

slides.narration_seed: Define What about Complete Coverage Path Planning (CCPP)? in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Coverage planning exists through the Coverage Server in opennav_coverage
It is not the basic “just load Nav2 and go” path for beginners
For this course, the practical order is: map → rooms → waypoints → keepout if needed
Coverage can be a bonus extension after your basic waypoint project works reliably
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What about Complete Coverage Path Planning (CCPP)? to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slide_ref.source_title: Recommended project path from today’s lecture

slides.narration_seed: Use Recommended project path from today’s lecture to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: 1) Use your saved SLAM map and reliable AMCL localization
2) Define room points or room-entry points in the map frame
3) Send multiple goals using Simple Commander waypoint logic
4) Add a keepout filter later if there are risky areas near furniture or entrances
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from What about Complete Coverage Path Planning (CCPP)? into Recommended project path from today’s lecture so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Recommended project path from today’s lecture sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slide_ref.source_title: What you should remember from this final lecture

slides.narration_seed: Use What you should remember from this final lecture as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: Nav2 = BT Navigator + task servers + costmaps + TF + localization + plugins
Planner ≠ controller; path generation and path tracking are different jobs
A saved map is not enough — costmaps and TF make navigation practical
Students should understand the major Nav2 components and what each contributes.
For your project, start with waypoints, not manual RViz clicking
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What you should remember from this final lecture to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
